name: Thinking Out of the Box
tagline: Some problems become easy only after you reframe an implicit constraint. The trick is noticing the constraint isn't there.

blocks.text: Most brain teasers in this category share a structure: a problem that LOOKS underconstrained or impossible because you've silently imported an assumption that isn't actually in the problem statement. The puzzle resolves the moment you challenge that assumption.

Examples of imported assumptions: 'each side of a die can only display ONE digit' (Calendar Cubes -- but 6 and 9 can share a face); 'I can ask each guard only about themselves' (Door to Offer -- but you can ask about what the OTHER would say); 'a light bulb has only one observable state' (Light Switches -- it's also hot or cold).

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: When stuck, list every assumption you're making. For each one, ask: 'is this in the problem, or did I add it?' The answer is often hiding in an assumption you can drop.

whenToUse: The problem feels impossible by direct attack.
You're an exact count or single value short of being able to solve it.
The problem hints at having a clever / surprising answer (interview problems often do).
Standard techniques (counting, recursion, probability) don't seem to apply.
recipe: Solve the problem under the obvious interpretation. Note where you get stuck.
List every assumption you've imported. Be paranoid -- include things that 'go without saying'.
For each assumption, ask: 'what if this weren't true? Is the rest of the problem still well-defined?'
If dropping an assumption opens a path, you've found the trick. Verify it satisfies everything explicitly stated.
commonPitfalls: Dropping assumptions that ARE actually in the problem -- read the statement again carefully.
Settling for the first 'creative' idea instead of confirming it's the simplest one that works.
Confusing 'the solution exists' with 'I've found it'. After spotting the trick, walk through every detail.
Telling the interviewer the trick without proving it satisfies all stated constraints.
